text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Sarah Ahmed
Riverside Health Centre

12 Mill Quay, Southwark, London SE1 2AB
Dear Dr Sarah Ahmed
Daniel Harper, DOB: 14-03-1987, Hospital No: H928417 NHS No. 7410295683
Mooring 5, Thames Path Houseboat, London SE10 0XY 

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures of unclear classification; history largely patient-reported. Prior routine EEG in 2022 reportedly inconclusive; no documented MRI on file. Lives on a houseboat/mobile home with intermittent power supply affecting device charging and access to reminders.

Current issues and history: I reviewed Daniel Harper in clinic today following a self-referral via the advice line, with collateral telephone input from his partner. He reports variability in sleep related to mooring location and occasional power outages, which interrupt use of his phone seizure app and medication alarms. He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 2 episodes weekly. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable. He denies injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence. Triggers suspected by the patient include sleep deprivation and missed doses during periods of power loss. No alcohol misuse; occasional caffeine late evening. Mood described as “steady though frustrated”, no self-harm thoughts. 

Medication history (patient-reported):
- Current: Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily; Clobazam 10 mg nocte PRN up to 3 nights/week for clusters (rare use). Vitamin D 1000 IU daily.
- Past tried/ceased: Levetiracetam (mood irritability), Sodium valproate (weight gain and tremor), Carbamazepine (dizziness), Lacosamide (no clear benefit).
- Not tried: Topiramate, Zonisamide, Pregabalin, Perampanel, Brivaracetam, Rufinamide.

Adherence/safety: He states generally good adherence, with two missed evening doses in the last month linked to power loss and failed alarms. He keeps medication in a dry box; no stockouts. Does not drive and is aware of DVLA obligations; last generalised seizure over 12 months ago but he remains cautious about boating-related risks. Safety advice discussed regarding water, ladders, and solo tasks on deck.

Examination (today): Alert, oriented, normal speech. No focal neurological deficits. Weight stable; BP 126/78, pulse 72 regular. Cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal examinations unremarkable. No cutaneous stigmata.

Investigations: No recent labs on file. I have requested U&Es, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D level, and lamotrigine trough level (to be done at local phlebotomy). I will also request a repeat routine EEG with sleep deprivation where feasible; consideration of ambulatory EEG if logistics allow. 

Social/contextual factors: Lives on a houseboat/mobile home; intermittent electricity impacts alarm reliability and device charging. We discussed low-tech prompts (paper chart, battery-powered alarm watch) and a spare power bank kept charged when docked. Partner provides oversight with a simple seizure log when present.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily for now. No dose change until labs/levels available and adherence supports optimisation.
- Reinforce adherence supports: provide written dosing chart, recommend battery alarm watch, and supply information on community power-bank grant scheme via neurology nurse.
- Clobazam: continue PRN as currently used; advised to avoid more than 3 consecutive nights without notifying the team.
- Arrange routine EEG; consider ambulatory monitoring if episodes cluster and logistics permit.
- Bloods as above; review results by telephone in 4–6 weeks with neurology nurse. If seizures persist at current frequency with therapeutic lamotrigine level and good adherence, discuss trial of an alternative add-on such as brivaracetam or zonisamide, balanced against prior tolerability.
- Seizure first-aid and water safety reviewed; advised to avoid lone work on deck when unwell or sleep-deprived; ensure partner aware of recovery positioning and when to seek urgent help (prolonged event >5 minutes, new injuries, or cluster not resolving).
- Information leaflet provided on medication storage in variable temperatures.

Follow-up: Nurse telephone review in 4–6 weeks after bloods and EEG scheduling. Clinic review in 4 months, sooner if frequency increases, any injury, or change in event semiology.

Please let me know if any issues arise in the interim. Thank you for your ongoing care and for assisting with local blood tests.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 2 episodes weekly. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 2 episodes weekly.' This provides a clear numeric frequency. There are no other higher-frequency seizure types reported. Generalised tonic-clonic seizures have not occurred since last year, which does not affect the current frequency as the focal seizures continue. Therefore, the normalized label is 2 per week.
reference: He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 2 episodes weekly. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable
